risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician at the University of California Davis stratified individual communities by selected indicators (population size; proximity to road and Bobo-Dioulasso; and health clinic affiliation) and computer-generated an assignment within strata to participate in the intervention cohort (IC; 25 communities) or in the non-intervention cohort (NIC; 9 communities). The same statistician, who was blinded to the intervention, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession for the enrollment of eligible infants in the IC." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random assignment within strata and a random allocation sequence at concession level, which are explicit indicators of a proper random sequence generation method.

_evidence.method_quote: A statistician at the University of California Davis stratified individual communities by selected indicators (population size; proximity to road and Bobo-Dioulasso; and health clinic affiliation) and computer-generated an assignment within strata to participate in the intervention cohort (IC; 25 communities) or in the non-intervention cohort (NIC; 9 communities). The same statistician, who was blinded to the intervention, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession for the enrollment of eligible infants in the IC.
_evidence.method_summary: Communities were stratified and assigned by computer-generated randomization; infants in intervention communities were assigned using a random allocation sequence at concession level.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The same statistician, who was blinded to the intervention, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession for the enrollment of eligible infants in the IC." Comment: This describes sequence generation and that the statistician was blinded, but it does not explicitly state how upcoming assignments were concealed from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is described, so allocation concealment remains unclear.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The same statistician, who was blinded to the intervention, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession for the enrollment of eligible infants in the IC.
_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation and masking, but gives no explicit method for concealing upcoming allocation from recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “The trial was partially masked, as all participants, field staff and researchers remained blinded to the four intervention groups until data analyses were completed, but were aware which communities were assigned to IC and NIC.” Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the intervention were explicitly reported as blinded to the four intervention groups, and the masking procedures (identical LNS except zinc content; identical zinc/placebo tablets; color-coded labels) support that blinding was likely maintained. Although some outcomes included morbidity measures, the study states that the intervention-group blinding was preserved until analysis, so performance bias is unlikely to have affected the dominant study outcomes overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “The trial was partially masked, as all participants, field staff and researchers remained blinded to the four intervention groups until data analyses were completed, but were aware which communities were assigned to IC and NIC.”
_evidence.blinding_method: “The LNS for each treatment group were identical, except for their zinc content.” “Zinc tablets were provided as water-dispersible tablets ... containing 5 mg zinc or an identical placebo.” “SQ-LNS and packages of tablets were labeled with one of eight color codes (two colors per intervention group).”

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was partially masked, as all participants, field staff and researchers remained blinded to the four intervention groups until data analyses were completed"; and the outcomes were measured as "Length, weight, MUAC and HC were measured" and laboratory outcomes were "analyzed by ELISA" / "pZn was determined by inductively-coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants, field staff, and researchers were blinded to the intervention groups during the trial, which indicates blinded outcome assessment for the reported outcomes. The main outcomes here were measured by anthropometrists or laboratory systems rather than subjective clinician judgment, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence measurement. Although diarrhea was caregiver-reported, the trial reports that caregivers and field staff were blinded to the intervention groups, so the self-reported morbidity outcome is not at high risk of detection bias.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The trial was partially masked, as all participants, field staff and researchers remained blinded to the four intervention groups until data analyses were completed, but were aware which communities were assigned to IC and NIC.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Among the four intervention groups, 19.5% dropped out by the final visit at 18 months. In the NIC, 15.2% were lost to anthropometric follow up at 18 months." Comment: The analysed outcome denominators are reported per arm (e.g. Table 5: 579, 593, 593, 599 vs 664), giving attrition of 23/602 (3.8%), 20/613 (3.3%), 10/603 (1.7%), 18/617 (2.9%) in the four intervention arms and 121/785 (15.4%) in NIC. Although NIC attrition is higher, the overall loss is moderate and the imbalance is about 10 percentage points, but the paper reports assessment of bias from loss to follow-up and states there were no differences in characteristics between completers and dropouts except maternal age. For the primary grouped comparison, this does not indicate clearly outcome-related missingness or problematic imputation.

per_arm_flow.reasons: lost to anthropometric follow up at 18 months

_evidence.notes: The article reports 19.5% dropout in the intervention groups and 15.2% lost to anthropometric follow up in NIC, with per-arm N in Table 5 for morbidity outcomes showing different analyzed denominators (579-599 vs 664). Reasons for loss were not described beyond 'lost to anthropometric follow up at 18 months' for NIC.
